introduction: Surds and Indices form the backbone of algebraic simplification in quantitative aptitude. Index laws let you manipulate powers efficiently, while surds deal with irrational roots that cannot be expressed as exact rationals. Mastery here speeds up simplification, comparison, and equation-solving across arithmetic and algebra.
explanation:
CONCEPT EXPLANATION

Indices (exponents) represent repeated multiplication. For base a > 0 and integers m, n: a^m × a^n = a^(m+n); a^m ÷ a^n = a^(m−n); (a^m)^n = a^(mn); (ab)^n = a^n b^n; a^0 = 1; a^(−n) = 1/a^n; a^(1/n) = ⁿ√a.

Surds are irrational roots such as √2, √3, ∛5. Like surds can be combined: 3√2 + 5√2 = 8√2. Unlike surds (√2 and √3) cannot be added directly. To rationalize a denominator containing √a, multiply numerator and denominator by √a (or the conjugate for binomial denominators).

Fractional indices bridge surds and powers: x^(1/2) = √x, x^(2/3) = (∛x)². Always express terms with the same base before applying index laws.

REAL-LIFE APPLICATIONS

Compound interest formulas use fractional and negative exponents. Engineering tolerances, signal decay, pH calculations, and scientific notation all rely on index manipulation. Surds appear in geometry (diagonal of a square) and physics formulas involving square roots.

COMMON MISTAKES

1) Adding exponents when bases differ: 2³ × 3² ≠ 6⁵.
2) Treating (a + b)^n as a^n + b^n.
3) Forgetting a^0 = 1 for a ≠ 0.
4) Incorrect rationalization: missing the conjugate for (√a − √b) denominators.
5) Simplifying √(a + b) as √a + √b.

IMPORTANT NOTES

• For comparisons, convert to the same base or compare logarithms/decimals.
• Surd multiplication: √a × √b = √(ab).
• (√a)² = a for a ≥ 0.
• Rationalize denominators to eliminate surds from the bottom fraction.
• Negative exponent means reciprocal: x^(−2) = 1/x².

formulas.title: Product law of indices
formulas.content: a^m × a^n = a^(m + n) for a > 0

formulas.title: Quotient law of indices
formulas.content: a^m ÷ a^n = a^(m − n) for a > 0

shortcutTips.title: Same base first
shortcutTips.tip: Rewrite all terms with a common base before applying index laws.

shortcutTips.title: Compare powers via exponents
shortcutTips.tip: For a > 1, larger exponent means larger value; for 0 < a < 1, the reverse holds.

shortcutTips.title: Rationalize quickly
shortcutTips.tip: For 1/√a, multiply top and bottom by √a to get √a/a.

shortcutTips.title: Zero exponent
shortcutTips.tip: Any non-zero number to power 0 equals 1 — spot this to simplify fast.

shortcutTips.title: Factor under the root
shortcutTips.tip: Pull out perfect squares from surds before combining like terms.
